evidence: All HTTP endpoints are public with no authentication or authorization layer — no route has an auth decorator, middleware, or permission check of any kind

CORS is the only access control mechanism: getCorsAllowOrigin() applies a uniform origin restriction to every endpoint response via setCorsHeader(exchange), called in sendJsonResponse, sendError, sendBinaryResponse, and sendOptionsResponse
The CORS origin is configurable at runtime via -Dcors.allow.origin JVM property, which is read at response-send time rather than at class-load — this is consistent and avoids stale configuration
No user sessions, no API keys, no JWT, no role-based permission strings — authorization is uniformly absent, not mixed

The server binds to InetAddress.getLoopbackAddress() (127.0.0.1) by default — a network-level access control that limits exposure to localhost; this is consistent across all endpoints since they share the same server instance

POST endpoints (/api/bcql, /api/visual/radial) accept arbitrary user-supplied patterns; BCQL has a length limit (MAX_BCQL_PATTERN_LENGTH=1024) and complexity limit (MAX_BCQL_BRACKET_DEPTH=20) as implicit input validation, but there is no analogous limit on the radial plot items array (capped at 40 items by client-side truncation)

strengths: CORS origin enforcement is applied uniformly to every response via a single setCorsHeader call — no endpoint can accidentally omit it
Loopback-only binding provides consistent network-level isolation for all endpoints simultaneously
The BCQL endpoint applies consistent input complexity limits (length and bracket depth) to both POST /api/bcql and indirectly protects the index from expensive queries
authorization_consistency.issue_character: This is a local corpus-analysis tool with no user accounts — the absence of authorization is consistent and appropriate for the deployment context; the one minor inconsistency is uneven input size constraints between POST endpoints.
authorization_consistency.score_rationale: Authorization consistency for a read-only local research tool is primarily about whether the access control approach is applied uniformly — and it is. All routes are intentionally unauthenticated, CORS is applied to every response, and the server binds to loopback. The slight deduction comes from the missing input size guard on the radial plot items (the BCQL endpoint has one, the radial endpoint does not) which creates an inconsistency in the defensive programming convention between sibling POST endpoints.

issues.summary: POST /api/visual/radial truncates items at 40 client-side but has no server-enforced body-size or item-count limit analogous to BCQL's complexity guards
evidence: VisualizationHandlers reads up to MAX_REQUEST_BODY_BYTES (65536) bytes — body size limit is present

Items array is truncated to 40 entries with `int limit = Math.min(40, itemsArr.size())` — this is a processing limit, not a rejection limit; a request with 10,000 items still passes the body size check and allocates itemsArr in memory before truncation
SketchHandlers.handleBcqlQueryPost explicitly rejects patterns exceeding MAX_BCQL_PATTERN_LENGTH with a 400 error; no equivalent rejection occurs for item count in radial
issues.suggestion: Add an explicit item count check before processing: if `itemsArr != null && itemsArr.size() > 40 + some_tolerance` reject with 400; this matches the BCQL complexity-limit convention and ensures consistent input validation across POST endpoints

root_causes: The BCQL endpoint received defensive limits as part of a focused security hardening pass; the radial endpoint was not reviewed in the same pass
possible_false_positives: The absence of user authentication is intentional for this deployment context and should not be flagged as an authorization_consistency defect
